title: Cesium Chloride Dialysis for Viruses
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"58jr5","text":"Version 3","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bcpr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a4jeq","text":"This protocol describes the use of Cesium Chloride to purify viruses with an optional dialysis step to remove the CsCl. DNase I treatment, CsCl purification, and sucrose purification methods were compared using replicated viral metagenomics in Hurwitz et al. 2012.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8choc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d5qdp","text":"References:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"UNDERLINE","offset":0,"length":11},{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3fhoe","text":"Hurwitz, B.L., Deng, L., Poulos, B.T., \u0026 Sullivan, M.B. (2012). Evaluation of methods to concentrate and purify ocean virus communities through comparative, replicated metagenomics. Environ Microbiol. 15(5), 1428–1440. doi:10.1111/j.1462-2920.2012.02836.x.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}

This is number (4) of (4):","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8khuj","text":"1. Cesium Chloride Gradients","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":3,"length":25}],"data":[]},{"key":"em600","text":"2. CsCl Step Gradient to Purify Phage","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":3,"length":34}],"data":[]},{"key":"fvo9t","text":"3. Cesium Chloride and DNA Extraction of Viruses using Wizard Prep Columns","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":2,"offset":3,"length":71}],"data":[]},{"key":"buk66","text":"4. Cesium Chloride Dialysis for Viruses","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":3,"offset":3,"length":36}],"data":[]},{"key":"bpj25","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7s7k6","text":"To prepare a CsCl solution of a particular density, the percent by weight of CsCl can be calculated by the formula:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"aalnm","text":"% wt/wt = 137.48 - 138.11/p","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"f05ik","text":"where 'p' is the desired density. For example, for p = 1.7 g/mL, use 56.24g CsCl and 43.76 mL H2O.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ehqfp","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"50k94","text":"Needed:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fe1r7","text":"Milli-Q Water","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"aac0u","text":"MTN 100 (NaCl, Tris-Cl (pH 7.5), MgCl2)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2uovq","text":"Slide-A-Lyzer dialysis casettes","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bqt06","text":"Tris Base","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2n4b1","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fktru","text":"If it is necessary to remove the CsCl, the virus preparation should be dialyzed against a buffer containing sodium chloride and magnesium chloride. The virus should be dialyzed against 4 changes of buffer with the first at a high salt concentration, then at an intermediate salt concentration and finally twice using the final salt concentration. Dialysis should be at 300x the sample volume for 30 min at room temperature. The dialysis membrane should have a 10,000 molecular weight cut-off (10K MWCO).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"28FD16F5854941ACA5BF0E3E8F740C26","url":"https://www.protocols.io/view/Cesium-Chloride-gradients-and-DNA-Extraction-Virus-c7dzi5"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"5AF57E4010164D029ABB76C0A439309C","url":"https://www.protocols.io/view/CsCl-Step-Gradient-to-Purify-Phage-c4zyx5"}},"2":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"1B43890FE42E410F8F7698A588F69887","url":"https://www.protocols.io/view/DNA-Extraction-of-Viruses-using-Wizard-Prep-Column-c26yhd"}},"3":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"B64D7FBDA28B4B8DB4828EA512B5EB71","url":"https://www.protocols.io/view/Cesium-Chloride-Dialysis-for-Viruses-c7jzkm?guidelines"}}}}
